title: Guidelines for band gap opening in graphene superlattices with periodic π-vacancy distribution
authors: Diyan Unmu Dzujah, Hongde Yu, Thomas Heine
abstract: Periodic $π$-vacancies in graphene superlattices (GSLs) provide a symmetry-based route to band-gap opening in graphene by modifying the $π$-band dispersion. However, the symmetry conditions that determine whether a vacancy motif can open a band gap remain unclear. Here, we investigate periodic $π$-vacancy GSLs using a nearest-neighbor tight-binding model with one $p_z$ orbital per carbon site to identify the symmetry requirements for gap opening. $π$-vacancies, representing functionalized, substituted, or missing carbon sites, are modeled as site deletions in the $π$ basis, with all hopping matrix elements to and from the deleted sites set to zero. We focus on $π$-vacancy motifs with $C_2$ and $C_3$ point-group symmetry. A $3n \times 3n$ GSL, where $n=1,2,3,\ldots$ is the integer scaling factor multiplying the honeycomb primitive-cell vectors, folds $K$ and $K'$ to $Γ$ and can therefore open a band gap. For $C_3$-type vacancies, the Dirac cones remain pinned at high-symmetry points and thus stay at $Γ$ in folded $3n$ GSLs. In contrast, $C_2$-type vacancies that reduce the global point group of the GSL to $D_{2h}$ by preserving a pair of perpendicular mirror symmetries, $σ_v \perp σ_d$, can also constrain the Dirac cones to $Γ$. When the $σ_v$ and $σ_d$ mirror planes are absent, the cones are allowed to shift away from $Γ$ to $(\pm Δq,\pm Δq)$ in the $3n$ superlattice.
summary: This paper establishes symmetry-based guidelines for creating band gaps in graphene superlattices via periodic vacancies. It demonstrates that C3-symmetric vacancy patterns are superior for producing large, stable gaps, whereas C2-symmetric patterns lead to smaller, anisotropic gaps and shifted Dirac cones.
main_problem: Identifying the symmetry-based design rules and guidelines for opening a robust band gap in graphene superlattices (GSLs) using periodic pi-vacancy distributions.
main_result: The study finds that 3n x 3n superlattices are required to fold K and K' to the Gamma point, and that C3-symmetric vacancy motifs are more efficient at opening large, isotropic gaps compared to C2-symmetric motifs which cause cone shifting and anisotropy.
method: Tight-binding simulations using the PythTB package, combined with symmetry group analysis and Taylor expansion of energy dispersion.
model_or_system: Graphene superlattices (GSLs) featuring periodic pi-vacancy distributions (modeled as site deletions) using a nearest-neighbor tight-binding model with one pz orbital per carbon site.
key_observables: Band gap magnitude, Dirac cone positions in momentum space, band degeneracy, and energy contour anisotropy (circular vs. elliptical).
important_parameters: Superlattice size (N x N), defect concentration, vacancy motif symmetry (C3 vs. C2), and sublattice imbalance.
main_assumptions: The model assumes a nearest-neighbor tight-binding approximation and focuses on cases with equal removal of A and B sublattices to avoid zero-energy modes.
figures_summary: Figures include Brillouin zone definitions, band structure plots for various vacancy configurations, energy contour plots of the valence band maximum, and linear regression of band gap versus defect concentration.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of band gap engineering in graphene, defines the vacancy motifs and superlattice geometry, presents symmetry analysis for gap opening, quantifies the impact of symmetry reduction and vacancy displacement, and concludes with design guidelines for large-gap GSLs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
